s: An autonomous AI scientist for computational imaging.
Watch a run go from a plain-language task to a fully-traced, verifiable result — plan, execute, and report, with the evidence attached at every step.
Describe the task in plain language.
It reads your task and auto-detects whether it's an imaging or a generic problem, then plans the right comparisons — you can even at-mention attached files as exact references.
The agent plans the experiment.
Before a single GPU job runs, the agent's entire plan is adversarially reviewed by three independent models.
Every step is traced.
Every step is captured in an append-only provenance graph, where each metric links straight back to the exact code and raw data that produced it.
Results become a traceable paper.
The results become a traceable paper where every number carries its raw arrays and a measurement residual, so every claim cites its own evidence.
Diffusion-prior deconvolution.
The learned prior recovers sub-diffraction structure the Wiener baseline blurs away, at the measured noise level.
Steer the run — it's a workspace, not a slideshow.
Try an action or type a prompt and the workspace responds live, with the run's goals tracked as you steer it.
Interactive demo — every agent reply is pre-scripted and every number is an illustrative placeholder.
Try an action or type a prompt, then press → to continue.
Graph.
Nodes are clickable — selecting one sets the Agent panel's context (demo).
Plan.
01 Forward-model check done.
02 Wiener baseline done.
03 ADMM-TV tuned on val done steered: high-noise emphasis (demo).
04 Validation-split evaluation done 1 sample flagged.
05 λ sweep on val done.
06 Re-run sample 3 (corrected scaling) done.
07 Freeze config → held-out test split → report + doc done.
Steps are clickable — selecting one sets the Agent panel's context (demo).
Report.
sweep landed: best λ frozen (demo).
Config frozen → held-out test split run once → report + doc emitted.
Every row carries a record id, demo-0000 … demo-0003 (mock ids; nothing was computed for this page).
* illustrative placeholders — not benchmark claims.
Doc.
The doc assembles once every goal is met.
Finish the tour to see the publication-style document.
The AgX team — assembled by the run's doc step (demo).
Abstract.
We reconstruct blurred, noisy microscopy images with an ADMM-TV solver tuned exclusively on a validation split and evaluated once on a held-out test split.
Every reported number in this demo document is an illustrative placeholder carrying its provenance record id; no real experimental values appear anywhere on this page.
The document itself — structure, figure, and verification trail — is what the run's final step assembles.
Setup.
Forward model A: known blur kernel with declared Gaussian noise; splits fixed before tuning (tune: val, report: test); record ids demo-0000 … demo-0003.
Method.
ADMM-TV with λ selected by a validation-only sweep (step 05); the selected λ was frozen before the test-split evaluation.
Results.
Validation diagnostics gated the run (the step-04 check flagged sample 3; the step-06 re-run fixed it); the reported metrics come from the one-time held-out test split (step 07).
Per-sample placeholders: PSNR XX.X (sample), SSIM 0.9X (sample); residual/noise 0.9×*–1.1×*.
Fig. 1 (demo).
Side-by-side placeholder (this one is a synthetic drawing) — the released doc embeds the same run's frozen arrays.
Verification & provenance.

Every row passed the residual gate (≤ 1.2× declared noise, placeholder values); in the released pipeline each figure and table cell cites its immutable record id and the records replay — here every id is fictional and no computation occurred.
Limitations.
This is a scripted demonstration document: its numbers are placeholders, its figure is synthetic, and nothing in it is a scientific claim.
The released pipeline assembles the same structure from sealed, human-approved evidence.
Agent.
Done — a publication-style report and doc, every number still an illustrative placeholder.
Scripted demo; enable JavaScript for the interactive tour.
Built to not fool itself.
Image metrics alone can never approve a result — a 3-agent verifier, measurement-space checks, and a human gate stand between a pretty picture and a claim.
